pathways.label: Arrest with no charges filed
pathways.summary: Arrest, no charges filed Eligible if the person was arrested, no charges of any type were filed, and either the statute of limitations expired or the prosecutor declined to file charges. Oklahoma uses the word expungement, but in most adult criminal-record contexts it means sealing, not automatic destruction. A Section 18/19 expungement can seal criminal records and related public civil records arising from the same arrest, transaction, or occurrence. Oklahoma now distinguishes between fully sealed records and partially sealed records. Fully sealed records are not available to the public or law enforcement, though OSBI may retain them for…
ruleClauses: Arrest, no charges filed Eligible if the person was arrested, no charges of any type were filed, and either the statute of limitations expired or the prosecutor declined to file charges.

Oklahoma uses the word expungement, but in most adult criminal-record contexts it means sealing, not automatic destruction. A Section 18/19 expungement can seal criminal records and related public civil records arising from the same arrest, transaction, or occurrence. Oklahoma now distinguishes between fully sealed records and partially sealed records. Fully sealed records are not available to the public or law enforcement, though OSBI may retain them for research/statistical purposes; partially sealed records are hidden from the public but remain available to law enforcement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")

OSBIs public guidance says a Section 18 expungement can expunge the entire arrest record, while a Section 991(c) expungement only updates a successfully completed deferred sentence so the case shows as dismissed; a 991(c) expungement does not remove the arrest record. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")
Full or broader arrest/case expungement 22 O.S. 18-19 Acquittals, dismissals, no-charge arrests, eligible misdemeanors, eligible felonies
4 Proof no adult arrest or pending adult charge.
waitingRules: Eligible if the person was charged with a misdemeanor, the charge was dismissed after successful completion of deferred judgment or delayed sentence, the person has never been convicted of a felony, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and at least 1 year has passed since dismissal. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")

Eligible if the person was charged with a nonviolent felony not listed in 57 O.S. 571, the charge was dismissed after successful completion of deferred judgment or delayed sentence, the person has never been convicted of a felony, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and at least 5 years have passed since dismissal. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")

OSBIs 2025 eligibility table also identifies a route for a person charged with not more than two felony offenses, where the charges were dismissed after successful completion of deferred judgment or delayed sentence, none were 85%-crime-type offenses under 21 O.S. 13.1 or sex-offender-registration offenses, no charges are pending, and at least 10 yearshave passed since dismissal.

Eligible if the person was convicted of a misdemeanor, received imprisonment, suspended sentence, or a fine greater than $500, has not been convicted of a felony, has no pending misdemeanor/felony charges, and at least 5 years have passed since the end of the last misdemeanor sentence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")

Eligible if the person was convicted of a nonviolent felony offense not listed in 57 O.S. 571, has not been convicted of another felony, has not been convicted of a separate misdemeanor in the last 7 years, has no pending misdemeanor/felony charges, and at least 5 years have passed since completion of the felony sentence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")

Eligible if the person was convicted of not more than two felony offenses, none of which is listed in 21 O.S. 13.1 and none requires sex-offender registration, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and at least 10 years have passed since completion of the felony sentence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
exclusionRules: All charges dismissed, not deferred Eligible if all misdemeanor/felony charges were dismissed, the person has never been convicted of a felony, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and the statute of limitations expired or the prosecutor confirms the charges will not be refiled. This route does not apply to charges dismissed after completion of a deferred judgment or delayed sentence. (

This route does not apply to charges dismissed after completion of a deferred judgment or delayed sentence. ( "https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/") Wilma status: likely_eligible_all_charges_dismissed_no_deferred B.

pathways.label: Deferred-sentence court-record expungement under § 991(c)
pathways.summary: Section 991(c) applies when the court deferred judgment and the person successfully completed all conditions. Upon completion and payment of ordered fines, fees, and monetary assessments, the person is discharged without a judgment of guilt, the plea is expunged from the record, and the charge is dismissed with prejudice. The clerk deletes the persons name from the docket sheet and public index, but a confidential index is maintained. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-991c/") Critical Wilma distinction:A 991(c) expungement is not the same as a Section 18/19 expungement. Upon completion and payment of ordered fines, fees, and monetary assessments, the person is discharged without a judgment of guilt, the plea is expunged from the record, and the charge is dismissed with prejudice. Critical Wilma distinction:A 991(c) expungement is not the same as a Section 18/19 expungement. OSBI says a 991(c) expungement updates the deferred case disposition to show dismissal but does not expunge/remove the arrest record. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")
Wilma status labels:eligible_991c_deferred_completedneeds_991c_completion_orderneeds_section_18_for_arrest_record

If you completed a deferred sentence in Oklahoma, the court may update the case so the plea is expunged and the case shows dismissed. But that alone usually does not remove the arrest record from OSBI. For that, we also screen Section 18/19 eligibility.

Oklahoma uses the word expungement, but in most adult criminal-record contexts it means sealing, not automatic destruction. A Section 18/19 expungement can seal criminal records and related public civil records arising from the same arrest, transaction, or occurrence. Oklahoma now distinguishes between fully sealed records and partially sealed records. Fully sealed records are not available to the public or law enforcement, though OSBI may retain them for research/statistical purposes; partially sealed records are hidden from the public but remain available to law enforcement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")

In Oklahoma, expungement usually means the record is sealed from public view. Some categories are fully sealed, but many are only partially sealed, meaning law enforcement can still access them. It does not always mean the record is physically destroyed.

OSBIs public guidance says a Section 18 expungement can expunge the entire arrest record, while a Section 991(c) expungement only updates a successfully completed deferred sentence so the case shows as dismissed; a 991(c) expungement does not remove the arrest record. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")

pathways.label: Clean Slate automatic expungement
pathways.summary: Oklahoma created a Clean Slate process for automatic sealing of certain arrest records. The statute directs OSBI to identify eligible records monthly, notify the prosecutor/arresting agency, allow a 45-day objection window, and then provide unobjectioned eligible cases to courts for signed expungement orders. Objections may include lack of eligibility, unpaid victim restitution, or supported belief that the person is continuing criminal activity. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-19/") OSBIs Clean Slate page says the law was created in 2022, funding and implementation have been phased, and there was not a clear…

arrest records. The statute directs OSBI to identify eligible records monthly, notify the prosecutor/arresting agency, allow a 45-day objection window, and then provide unobjectioned eligible cases to courts for signed expungement orders. Objections may include lack of eligibility, unpaid victim restitution, or supported belief that the person is continuing criminal activity. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-19/")

OSBIs Clean Slate page says the law was created in 2022, funding and implementation have been phased, and there was not a clear implementation deadline at the time of OSBIs posted update. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-10a/section-10a-2-6-109/") If granted, the court orders juvenile court records and related law-enforcement files sealed. The record is deemed never to have occurred,…
ruleClauses: A juvenile record subject, parent, guardian, or attorney may petition the district court where the juvenile record is located. The person must have completed the informal adjustment, deferred adjudication, probation, or custody requirements; the case must be dismissed/closing; there must be no adult arrest, charge, indictment, or information pending; and all court costs, restitution, fines, and ordered requirements must be completed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-10a/section-10a-2-6-109/")

If granted, the court orders juvenile court records and related law-enforcement files sealed. The record is deemed never to have occurred, and employers, schools, landlords, and government agencies may not require disclosure. However, juvenile/criminal justice actors may access the records for later juvenile/adult proceedings, sentencing, or placement, and records are not physically destroyed by default. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-10a/section-10a-2-6-109/")

when.sourceConditionText: 4 Proposed order dismissing case and expunging plea from court record.

Oklahoma uses the word expungement, but in most adult criminal-record contexts it means sealing, not automatic destruction. A Section 18/19 expungement can seal criminal records and related public civil records arising from the same arrest, transaction, or occurrence. Oklahoma now distinguishes between fully sealed records and partially sealed records. Fully sealed records are not available to the public or law enforcement, though OSBI may retain them for research/statistical purposes; partially sealed records are hidden from the public but remain available to law enforcement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Clean Slate automatic sealing 22 O.S. 18(C), 19(B) Some eligible records, implementation dependent

In Oklahoma, expungement usually means the record is sealed from public view. Some categories are fully sealed, but many are only partially sealed, meaning law enforcement can still access them. It does not always mean the record is physically destroyed.

OSBIs public guidance says a Section 18 expungement can expunge the entire arrest record, while a Section 991(c) expungement only updates a successfully completed deferred sentence so the case shows as dismissed; a 991(c) expungement does not remove the arrest record. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")

copyGuardrails: Oklahoma uses the word expungement, but in most adult criminal-record contexts it means sealing, not automatic destruction.
It does not always mean the record is physically destroyed.

OSBIs public guidance says a Section 18 expungement can expunge the entire arrest record, while a Section 991(c) expungement only updates a successfully completed deferred sentence so the case shows as dismissed; a 991(c) expungement does not remove the arrest record. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")
Use Oklahoma-specific remedy terminology returned by the profile; do not relabel every remedy as expungement.
Use “may qualify,” “possible path,” and “the court or agency makes the final decision.”
Do not promise erasure, sealing, eligibility, timing, filing acceptance, or a court outcome.
Do not show checkout unless the backend returns a packet-ready result and paymentAllowed=true.

sourceSections.title: Oklahomas core rule
sourceSections.text:
Oklahoma uses the word expungement, but in most adult criminal-record contexts it means sealing, not automatic destruction. A Section 18/19 expungement can seal criminal records and related public civil records arising from the same arrest, transaction, or occurrence. Oklahoma now distinguishes between fully sealed records and partially sealed records. Fully sealed records are not available to the public or law enforcement, though OSBI may retain them for research/statistical purposes; partially sealed records are hidden from the public but remain available to law enforcement. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma plain-language script:
In Oklahoma, expungement usually means the record is sealed from public view. Some categories are fully sealed, but many are only partially sealed, meaning law enforcement can still access them. It does not always mean the record is physically destroyed.

OSBIs public guidance says a Section 18 expungement can expunge the entire arrest record, while a Section 991(c) expungement only updates a successfully completed deferred sentence so the case shows as dismissed; a 991(c) expungement does not remove the arrest record. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")

sourceSections.title: Main Oklahoma pathways
sourceSections.text:
Pathway Authority Best used for
Full or broader arrest/case expungement 22 O.S. 18-19 Acquittals, dismissals, no-charge arrests, eligible misdemeanors, eligible felonies
Deferred sentence court-record expungement 22 O.S. 991c Completed deferred sentence; court plea/docket update only
Identity-theft / wrong-person record 22 O.S. 19a and 18 identity-theft category Someone else used the persons name/ID
Human-trafficking survivor relief 22 O.S. 19c Prostitution-related records tied to trafficking victimization
Victim protective order sealing 22 O.S. 60.18 Certain VPO court records
Juvenile record expungement 10A O.S. 2-6-109 Juvenile court records after completion/dismissal
Clean Slate automatic sealing 22 O.S. 18(C), 19(B) Some eligible records, implementation dependent

sourceSections.title: Section 18/19 adult expungement categories
sourceSections.text: Wilma should screen by pathway description, not just paragraph number, because Oklahoma has had recent amendments and some public sources show versioning/numbering differences. OSBIs 2025 Clean Slate eligibility table and current statutory compilations identify these major categories.

sourceSections.title: No conviction / innocence / dismissal routes
sourceSections.text:
1. Acquittal
Eligible if the person was acquitted.
Wilma status: likely_eligible_acquittal

sourceSections.title: Conviction reversed and dismissed
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if the conviction was reversed with instructions to dismiss, or reversed and the prosecutor later dismissed the charge.
Wilma status: likely_eligible_reversed_and_dismissed

sourceSections.title: DNA factual innocence
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if factual innocence was established by DNA evidence after conviction.
Wilma status: likely_eligible_dna_exoneration

sourceSections.title: All charges dismissed, not deferred
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if all misdemeanor/felony charges were dismissed, the person has never been convicted of a felony, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and the statute of limitations expired or the prosecutor confirms the charges will not be refiled. This route does not apply to charges dismissed after completion of a deferred judgment or delayed sentence. (

"https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_all_charges_dismissed_no_deferred

sourceSections.title: Misdemeanor deferred dismissal
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if the person was charged with a misdemeanor, the charge was dismissed after successful completion of deferred judgment or delayed sentence, the person has never been convicted of a felony, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and at least 1 year has passed since dismissal. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_misdemeanor_deferred_dismissal_1_year

sourceSections.title: Nonviolent felony deferred dismissal
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if the person was charged with a nonviolent felony not listed in 57 O.S. 571, the charge was dismissed after successful completion of deferred judgment or delayed sentence, the person has never been convicted of a felony, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and at least 5 years have passed since dismissal. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_nonviolent_felony_deferred_dismissal_5_years

sourceSections.title: Up to two felony deferred dismissals
sourceSections.text:
OSBIs 2025 eligibility table also identifies a route for a person charged with not more than two felony offenses, where the charges were dismissed after successful completion of deferred judgment or delayed sentence, none were 85%-crime-type offenses under 21 O.S. 13.1 or sex-offender-registration offenses, no charges are pending, and at least 10 yearshave passed since dismissal.
Wilma status: legal_review_possible_two_felony_deferred_dismissals_10_years
Wilma caution:
This is a technical Oklahoma category. Do not rely only on paragraph numbering; verify the exact current statute, charge list, and local practice before packet generation.

sourceSections.title: Other misdemeanor conviction
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if the person was convicted of a misdemeanor, received imprisonment, suspended sentence, or a fine greater than $500, has not been convicted of a felony, has no pending misdemeanor/felony charges, and at least 5 years have passed since the end of the last misdemeanor sentence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_misdemeanor_conviction_5_years

sourceSections.title: One nonviolent felony conviction
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if the person was convicted of a nonviolent felony offense not listed in 57 O.S. 571, has not been convicted of another felony, has not been convicted of a separate misdemeanor in the last 7 years, has no pending misdemeanor/felony charges, and at least 5 years have passed since completion of the felony sentence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_one_nonviolent_felony_5_years

sourceSections.title: Not more than two felony convictions
sourceSections.text:
Eligible if the person was convicted of not more than two felony offenses, none of which is listed in 21 O.S. 13.1 and none requires sex-offender registration, no misdemeanor/felony charges are pending, and at least 10 years have passed since completion of the felony sentence. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma status: likely_eligible_up_to_two_felonies_10_years

sourceSections.title: Full pardon by the Governor
sourceSections.text:
A person who received a full pardon by the Governor for the crime may seek expungement. A person under 18 at the time of the offense who received a full pardon also has a separate eligibility category. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-18v2/")
Wilma status labels:possible_full_pardon_expungementpossible_juvenile_pardon_expungement
Wilma caution:
A pardon and an expungement are not the same thing. A pardon may open an expungement pathway, but Wilma should still route the user through the Section 18/19 petition process.

sourceSections.title: Section 991(c): deferred sentence court-record expungement
sourceSections.text:
Section 991(c) applies when the court deferred judgment and the person successfully completed all conditions. Upon completion and payment of ordered fines, fees, and monetary assessments, the person is discharged without a judgment of guilt, the plea is expunged from the record, and the charge is dismissed with prejudice. The clerk deletes the persons name from the docket sheet and public index, but a confidential index is maintained. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-991c/")
Critical Wilma distinction:A 991(c) expungement is not the same as a Section 18/19 expungement. OSBI says a 991(c) expungement updates the deferred case disposition to show dismissal but does not expunge/remove the arrest record. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")
Wilma status labels:eligible_991c_deferred_completedneeds_991c_completion_orderneeds_section_18_for_arrest_record
Wilma script:
If you completed a deferred sentence in Oklahoma, the court may update the case so the plea is expunged and the case shows dismissed. But that alone usually does not remove the arrest record from OSBI. For that, we also screen Section 18/19 eligibility.

sourceSections.title: Clean Slate automatic expungement
sourceSections.text:
Oklahoma created a Clean Slate process for automatic sealing of certain
clean slate eligible
arrest records. The statute directs OSBI to identify eligible records monthly, notify the prosecutor/arresting agency, allow a 45-day objection window, and then provide unobjectioned eligible cases to courts for signed expungement orders. Objections may include lack of eligibility, unpaid victim restitution, or supported belief that the person is continuing criminal activity. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-22/section-22-19/")
OSBIs Clean Slate page says the law was created in 2022, funding and implementation have been phased, and there was not a clear implementation deadline at the time of OSBIs posted update. OSBI describes an implementation plan bridging the current petition-based process and the future Clean Slate process. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/clean-slate-initiative.html")
Wilma rule:Do not promise automatic expungement. Say:
This may be Clean Slate eligible, but if it has not happened automatically, you may still be able to file a petition.

Wilma status labels:possible_clean_slate_eligibleclean_slate_not_guaranteed_verify_osbipetition_available_if_auto_not_completed

sourceSections.title: Human-trafficking survivor relief
sourceSections.text:
Oklahoma has a trafficking-survivor pathway under 22 O.S. 19c for prostitution-related records when the offense was committed as a result of the person being a victim of human trafficking. Relief can be by court motion or petition, and it is narrower than general expungement but can avoid some ordinary timing/prior-record barriers. ("https://ccresourcecenter.org/state-restoration-profiles/oklahoma-restoration-of-rights-pardon-expungement-sealing/")
Wilma status: legal_aid_handoff_human_trafficking_survivor
Wilma script:
Oklahoma has a special expungement pathway for certain prostitution-related records connected to human trafficking victimization. That should be routed to legal aid or an attorney because the petition needs sensitive facts and supporting proof.

sourceSections.title: Juvenile record expungement
sourceSections.text:
A juvenile record subject, parent, guardian, or attorney may petition the district court where the juvenile record is located. The person must have completed the informal adjustment, deferred adjudication, probation, or custody requirements; the case must be dismissed/closing; there must be no adult arrest, charge, indictment, or information pending; and all court costs, restitution, fines, and ordered requirements must be completed. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-10a/section-10a-2-6-109/")
If granted, the court orders juvenile court records and related law-enforcement files sealed. The record is deemed never to have occurred, and employers, schools, landlords, and government agencies may not require disclosure. However, juvenile/criminal justice actors may access the records for later juvenile/adult proceedings, sentencing, or placement, and records are not physically destroyed by default. ("https://law.justia.com/codes/oklahoma/title-10a/section-10a-2-6-109/")
Wilma status labels:possible_juvenile_expungementnot_eligible_juvenile_adult_arrest_or_pending_chargeneeds_juvenile_completion_and_dismissal_proof

sourceSections.title: Required Wilma intake checklist
sourceSections.text:
Identity
Full legal name.
Prior names / aliases.
Date of birth.
Social Security number or last four digits.
Current address.
Phone and email.
Oklahoma SID number, if known.
Drivers license number, if traffic/DUI-related.
Arrest/case data
County of arrest.
Court/county where case was filed.
Case number.
Arrest date.
Arresting agency.
Prosecuting agency.
Original charge.
Final charge.
Disposition.
Date of dismissal, acquittal, conviction, pardon, reversal, or sentence completion.
Whether charges were dismissed after deferred judgment or delayed sentence.
Whether the case was never filed.
Whether prosecutor declined charges or confirms no refiling.
Whether statute of limitations has expired.
Conviction-specific data
Misdemeanor or felony.
Fine amount.
Jail/prison term.
Suspended sentence.
Deferred sentence.
Sentence-completion date.
Restitution status.
Treatment-program completion.
Whether the offense was later reclassified as a misdemeanor.
Whether any felony conviction exists.
Whether any misdemeanor/felony charge is pending.
Separate misdemeanor convictions in the last seven years.
Number of felony convictions.
Whether offenses arose from the same transaction or occurrence.
Exclusion / legal-review flags
57 O.S. 571 violent felony issue.
21 O.S. 13.1 serious/85%-crime issue.
Sex-offender registration.
Domestic violence / protective-order-related records.
Human trafficking victimization.
Identity theft / wrong-person facts.
Juvenile record.
Federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state record.
Immigration, firearm, licensing, school, healthcare, childcare, or security-clearance concerns.
OSBI asks that expungement submissions include the persons name, Social Security number, date of birth, charged crimes, arrest date, and disposition/end result. For domestic-related charges, OSBI asks for a police narrative/report showing the relationship to the victim to avoid processing delays. ("https://oklahoma.gov/osbi/services/information-services-division/disposition-services-unit/criminal-history-record-expungement.html")

sourceSections.title: Packet logic for RCAP/Wilma
sourceSections.text:
Section 18/19 packet
Generate/check:
1 Petition to Expunge Records Pursuant to 22 O.S. 18 and 19
2 Order Setting Hearing
3 Proposed Order to Expunge Records Pursuant to 22 O.S. 18 and 19
4 Certified docket/disposition.
5 Arrest record / OSBI record.
6 Proof of sentence completion, if conviction.
7 Proof restitution paid, if required.
8 Proof treatment completion, if reclassified felony.
9 Prosecutor no-refile confirmation, if dismissal route depends on it.
10 Service/notice to district attorney, arresting agency, OSBI, and any agency holding records.
11 Certified copies of signed order for OSBI and local agencies.
991(c) deferred sentence packet
Generate/check:
1 Motion or application to close/defer-expunge court record under 22 O.S. 991c.
2 Order showing deferred sentence completed.
3 Proof fines, fees, assessments paid or waived.
4 Proposed order dismissing case and expunging plea from court record.
5 Separate Section 18/19 screening for the arrest record.
Identity theft packet
Generate/check:
1 Petition/motion under 22 O.S. 19a or Section 18 identity-theft category.
2 Proof another person used the users name/identification.
3 Dismissal order based on identity misuse, if available.
4 Proposed order stating expungement is under 19a.
VPO packet
Generate/check:
1 Petition to expunge/seal VPO court record under 22 O.S. 60.18.
2 Proof of eligible category: dismissal/denial/failure to appear, vacatur date, or death.
3 Certified-mail notice to other party within statutory deadline.
4 Proposed order sealing from public inspection.
Juvenile packet
Generate/check:
1 Oral or written petition under 10A O.S. 2-6-109.
2 Proof completion of informal adjustment/deferred adjudication/probation/custody.
3 Proof dismissal or case closure.
4 Proof no adult arrest or pending adult charge.
5 Proof all costs, restitution, fines, and ordered requirements completed.
6 Proposed order to agencies/clerk/juvenile bureau/OJA/OSBI.
